title: Euclid NISP Spectral-Contamination Reliability Audit
question: How do released contamination indicators relate to continuum S/N, emission-line recovery and redshift reliability in selected NISP spectra?
gap: An external audit; not a decontamination, extraction or redshift pipeline.
dataMode: real public Euclid Q1 NISP products
novelty: An external audit of released Euclid Q1 NISP contamination indicators against continuum S/N, line recovery and redshift reliability; not a decontamination, extraction or redshift pipeline, and no causal claim is made from these descriptive catalogue correlations.
methodology: Euclid Q1 NISP spectroscopy products are queried live from IRSA's public Euclid Q1 TAP mirror (astroquery.ipac.irsa.Irsa), joining the SPE quality-flag table, the SPE galaxy redshift-candidate table and the MER positional catalogue on object_id, restricted to a single Q1 tile to keep queries fast and deterministic. The released spe_context_warning_flag / spe_context_error_flag columns are used as the operational contamination indicator (documented assumption: the SIR processing paper states the pipeline subtracts cross- and self-contamination, and these are the only released per-object flags plausibly tied to that step; their exact bit semantics are not published). Objects are split into clean/flagged groups and compared on: continuum S/N (spe_cont_snr, bootstrap CI), line-recovery rate (fraction with best Gaussian-fit line S/N spe_line_snr_gf above a threshold, swept across thresholds), and redshift reliability (spe_z_rel, spe_z_err). A synthetic-continuum line-injection/recovery gate (Gaussian line injected into a real or synthetic continuum, recovered via a matched Gaussian fit) validates the line-measurement machinery before any real-data conclusion is drawn. A permuted-label negative control and a 3-way threshold-sensitivity check (varying which flags count as 'contaminated') are run alongside the headline result.
assumptions: The released spe_context_warning_flag / spe_context_error_flag columns are used as the contamination indicator; their precise bit dictionary is not published in the queryable IRSA TAP column metadata, so this mapping is a documented, named assumption rather than an internal Euclid Consortium definition.
Archive metadata and calibration versions (Q1 SIR/PHZ processing) are recorded and interpreted as released, without independent recalibration.
Selection is deterministic: a single Q1 tile, ORDER BY object_id, TOP-N sampling, with no manual row-level curation.
Uncertainty is reported via bootstrap resampling (continuum S/N, outlier fraction) or fit-covariance convergence diagnostics (line injection), never conflated with each other.
limitations: An external audit of released catalogue products; not a decontamination, extraction or redshift pipeline, and not a replacement for the official Euclid SIR/PHZ processing.

Real-data result is a documented null finding, not a group comparison: on tile 102160061 (RGS grism), every released SPE quality flag examined (spe_context_warning_flag, spe_context_error_flag, spe_error_flag) is constant across all 6000 objects with a rank-0 galaxy redshift candidate, so no clean/flagged split, threshold-sensitivity check or negative control could be computed against real data in this release. This is reported transparently (see results/warnings.json) rather than substituted with a synthetic or partial split; the synthetic validation gate with a known injected effect still fully passes all 5 required checks.
Results apply only to the selected Q1 tile, RGS grism, and object selection documented in data/manifest.csv; they are not a survey-wide statement.

Real Q1 NISP spectroscopic redshifts have no independent ground truth available in this bounded release, so the real-data 'redshift reliability' metric uses the released spe_z_rel score and spe_z_err, not a truth-based catastrophic-failure outlier fraction (that stronger test is only possible on the synthetic validation gate, where a true redshift is known by construction).
Only 23 of 40 attempted real spectrum downloads succeeded (remainder skipped on transient IRSA 502 errors, recorded as warnings in data/manifest.csv provenance, not silently dropped).
Correlated errors, calibration systematics across detectors/tiles, and selection effects in which objects even receive a context flag are not modelled.

citation.author: Biswajit Jana
